behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real filesystem watching (recursive/nested path event delivery), which is OS- and watcher-backend-dependent. A mocked emitter can hide missed recursive events (MOCK-HIDE). Higher levels are disqualified: behavior has no AI/ML output dependence and no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real daemon against temp input/output dirs, create a nested subdirectory under the watched root, write a file there, and assert exactly one transformed output is produced from that nested path.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: Behavior text is explicitly about deduplication logic, which can be validated at function/object boundary with injected clock/time window and path keys; no OS/network/subprocess required. Rejected higher levels because the behavior does not require real filesystem events or model output.
behaviors.test_description: Call dedup component twice with same canonical file path within the configured window (using fake clock), assert first request is accepted and second is rejected; advance clock past window and assert acceptance again.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly depends on real OS duplicate event patterns for one write (REAL-INFRA). In-process mocks typically emit idealized events and can miss this failure mode (MOCK-HIDE). Higher levels disqualified: no AI/ML-dependent correctness, no multi-agent chain.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon on a real temp directory, perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple FS events (create+append/rename), and assert exactly one pipeline execution/output for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This is an OS-level watcher lifecycle/inode replacement scenario; correctness appears only with real directory delete/recreate semantics while process stays alive. Mocked watcher callbacks can falsely pass. Higher levels are disqualified by lack of AI/ML dependency and multi-step agent pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon, delete watched directory, recreate it with same path, then add a new file and verify it is processed without restarting the daemon process.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering depends on real file metadata timestamps plus concurrent arrival/watcher scheduling. Integration tests with injected timestamps can validate sorter logic, but can miss real timestamp granularity/availability and event timing interactions. Higher levels disqualified: no AI/ML decision quality or multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Create multiple files near-simultaneously in watched directory, record their actual creation timestamps from filesystem metadata, and assert processing order matches timestamp order (with explicit tie policy if timestamps are equal).

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Failure appears at real FS event timing boundary (arrival before write completion). Requires real partial-write behavior and watcher timing; mocks usually hide read-while-writing races. Higher levels disqualified because behavior is infrastructure/timing only, not AI/ML output dependent and not multi-agent.
behaviors.test_description: Open a file for streaming write in the watched directory, emit data chunks with delay, and verify daemon does not process until writer closes (or file stability condition is met), then processes exactly once.
